1.1.1.title: CPU Utilization Trending
1.1.1.value: Detects overloaded hosts before they cause application degradation. Enables capacity planning and right-sizing.

1.1.2.title: Memory Pressure Detection
1.1.2.value: Prevents OOM kills, application crashes, and unresponsive systems by detecting memory exhaustion early.

1.1.3.title: Disk Capacity Forecasting
1.1.3.value: Prevents outages caused by full filesystems. A full /var or / can bring down services, databases, and logging. Enables proactive storage procurement.

1.1.4.title: Disk I/O Saturation
1.1.4.value: High I/O wait degrades application performance even when CPU and memory look healthy. Catches storage bottlenecks before users complain.

1.1.5.title: System Load Anomalies
1.1.5.value: Load average exceeding CPU core count indicates process queuing. Useful as an early warning for runaway processes or unexpected workloads.

1.1.6.title: Process Crash Detection
1.1.6.value: Immediate awareness of unexpected process terminations. Critical for services that don't auto-restart or have no watchdog.

1.1.7.title: OOM Killer Events
1.1.7.value: OOM killer invocations mean the system ran out of memory and Linux chose to kill a process to survive. This often takes out critical services silently.

1.1.8.title: SSH Brute-Force Detection
1.1.8.value: Detects active password-guessing attacks against SSH services. Can be early indicator of compromised credentials or targeted intrusion attempts.

1.1.9.title: Unauthorized Sudo Usage
1.1.9.value: Monitors privilege escalation attempts. Failed sudo attempts may indicate an attacker exploring what a compromised account can do.

1.1.10.title: Cron Job Failure Monitoring
1.1.10.value: Failed cron jobs can silently break batch processing, backups, log rotation, and maintenance tasks. Catching failures early prevents cascading issues.

1.1.11.title: Kernel Panic Detection
1.1.11.value: Kernel panics cause immediate system crashes and potential data corruption. Often indicates hardware failure, driver issues, or memory corruption.

1.1.12.title: NTP Time Sync Drift
1.1.12.value: Clock drift causes authentication failures (Kerberos), log correlation issues, transaction ordering problems, and certificate validation failures.

1.1.13.title: Zombie Process Accumulation
1.1.13.value: Zombie processes indicate parent processes not properly reaping children. Accumulation can exhaust PID space and indicates application bugs.

1.1.14.title: File Descriptor Exhaustion
1.1.14.value: File descriptor exhaustion causes "too many open files" errors, breaking network connections, log writing, and inter-process communication. Common in Java apps and databases.

1.1.15.title: Network Interface Errors
1.1.15.value: Interface errors (CRC, drops, overruns) indicate bad cables, failing NICs, or duplex mismatches. Catching early prevents intermittent application failures.

1.1.16.title: Package Vulnerability Tracking
1.1.16.value: Maintains visibility into known vulnerable packages across the fleet, supporting vulnerability management and compliance programs.

1.1.17.title: Service Availability Monitoring
1.1.17.value: Detects stopped services before users notice. Essential for any SLA-bound service where uptime matters.

1.1.18.title: User Account Changes
1.1.18.value: Detects unauthorized user creation or modification. Key for security auditing and compliance (SOX, PCI, HIPAA).

1.1.19.title: Filesystem Read-Only Detection
1.1.19.value: A filesystem remounting as read-only indicates disk failure, corruption, or mount issues. Applications will fail silently when they can't write.

1.1.20.title: Reboot Detection
1.1.20.value: Unexpected reboots may indicate kernel panics, hardware failure, or unauthorized changes. Distinguishing planned vs. unplanned reboots is key.

1.1.21.title: Kernel Module Loading Tracking
1.1.21.value: Detects unauthorized kernel module insertions which can indicate rootkits or malware persistence.

1.1.22.title: Sysctl Parameter Changes Detection
1.1.22.value: Identifies modifications to kernel parameters that affect system behavior, security posture, or performance tuning.

1.1.23.title: Kernel Core Dump Generation
1.1.23.value: Core dumps indicate process crashes at kernel level, enabling root cause analysis of system stability issues.

1.1.24.title: Kernel Ring Buffer Error Rate
1.1.24.value: Ring buffer errors signal kernel-level problems including driver issues, hardware failures, or module conflicts.

1.1.25.title: NUMA Imbalance Detection
1.1.25.value: NUMA imbalance causes memory locality issues and performance degradation on multi-socket systems.

1.1.26.title: CPU Frequency Scaling Events
1.1.26.value: Frequency scaling changes indicate thermal throttling or power management adjustments affecting workload performance.

1.1.27.title: CPU Steal Time Elevation (Virtual Machines)
1.1.27.value: High steal time indicates VM is contending with host resources, affecting application performance.

1.1.28.title: IRQ Imbalance Across CPU Cores
1.1.28.value: Imbalanced IRQ handling causes uneven CPU utilization and can bottleneck network or storage throughput.

1.1.29.title: Context Switch Rate Anomaly Detection
1.1.29.value: Excessive context switching reduces CPU cache effectiveness and indicates scheduler overload or contention.

1.1.30.title: Scheduler Latency and Run Queue Depth
1.1.30.value: High run queue depth with elevated scheduling latency causes visible application performance degradation.

1.1.31.title: Hugepage Allocation and Usage
1.1.31.value: Hugepage contention or allocation failures impact database and large memory workload performance.

1.1.32.title: Transparent Hugepage Compaction Stalls
1.1.32.value: THP compaction stalls indicate severe memory fragmentation affecting latency-sensitive workloads.

1.1.33.title: Inode Exhaustion Detection
1.1.33.value: Inode exhaustion causes file creation failures even when disk space remains available, stopping applications.

1.1.34.title: RAID Array Degradation Detection
1.1.34.value: Degraded RAID arrays mean data loss risk and potential performance impact during rebuild.

1.1.35.title: LVM Thin Pool Capacity Monitoring
1.1.35.value: Thin pool exhaustion causes I/O errors on all logical volumes in the pool, causing application failures.

1.1.36.title: Multipath I/O Failover Events
1.1.36.value: Multipath failovers indicate storage path degradation requiring immediate investigation to prevent I/O loss.

1.1.37.title: NFS Mount Stale Handle Detection
1.1.37.value: Stale NFS handles cause application hangs and I/O failures that severely impact users.

1.1.38.title: Filesystem Journal Errors
1.1.38.value: Filesystem journal errors indicate potential corruption risk and can lead to data loss or recovery timeouts.

1.1.39.title: Ext4 Filesystem Errors and Recovery
1.1.39.value: Ext4 errors may indicate filesystem corruption or hardware issues requiring immediate diagnostic action.

1.1.40.title: XFS Filesystem Errors and Recovery
1.1.40.value: XFS errors indicate potential corruption in high-performance storage systems commonly used in enterprise environments.

1.1.41.title: Disk SMART Health Monitoring
1.1.41.value: SMART errors predict disk failure, enabling proactive replacement before data loss occurs.

1.1.42.title: SSD Wear Leveling and Health
1.1.42.value: SSD wear metrics indicate remaining lifespan, enabling proactive replacement planning.

1.1.43.title: Fstrim and TRIM Command Monitoring
1.1.43.value: Fstrim failures indicate potential SSD performance degradation from lack of proper space reclamation.

1.1.44.title: Memory Leak Detection Per Process
1.1.44.value: Process memory leaks cause gradual performance degradation and eventual OOM situations.

1.1.45.title: Swap Thrashing Detection
1.1.45.value: Swap thrashing causes severe performance degradation and can make systems unresponsive.

1.1.46.title: Slab Cache Growth Monitoring
1.1.46.value: Unbounded slab cache growth consumes memory that could be used for page cache or application memory.

1.1.47.title: Page Cache Pressure and Reclaim Activity
1.1.47.value: High page cache reclaim activity indicates memory pressure affecting application performance.

1.1.48.title: NUMA Memory Imbalance Per Node
1.1.48.value: NUMA memory imbalance causes remote memory access latency affecting NUMA-aware applications.

1.1.49.title: Memory Cgroup Limit Enforcement
1.1.49.value: Cgroup limits prevent runaway processes but enforcement indicates containers at memory limits need scaling.

1.1.50.title: Transparent Hugepage Defragmentation Stalls
1.1.50.value: THP defrag stalls cause application latency spikes affecting real-time and interactive workloads.

1.1.51.title: TCP Retransmission Rate Elevation
1.1.51.value: High retransmission rates indicate network congestion, packet loss, or application issues affecting throughput.

1.1.52.title: Connection Tracking Table Exhaustion
1.1.52.value: Conntrack table full prevents new network connections, causing application failures.

1.1.53.title: Socket Buffer Overflow Detection
1.1.53.value: Socket buffer overflows cause packet drops and connection resets, indicating network saturation or misconfiguration.

1.1.54.title: Network Namespace Monitoring
1.1.54.value: Network namespace monitoring detects container escape attempts and validates network isolation in containerized environments.

1.1.55.title: DNS Resolution Failure Rate
1.1.55.value: DNS failures impact application availability and user experience, requiring immediate investigation.

1.1.56.title: Firewall Rule Hit Tracking (iptables/nftables)
1.1.56.value: Firewall rule tracking identifies blocked traffic patterns, helping optimize rules and detect attack attempts.

1.1.57.title: ARP Table Overflow Detection
1.1.57.value: ARP table overflow causes network connectivity issues and may indicate ARP spoofing attacks or network misconfiguration.

1.1.58.title: Network Bond Failover Events
1.1.58.value: Network bond failovers indicate NIC or port failures requiring immediate remediation to prevent connectivity loss.

1.1.59.title: Network Team Failover Detection
1.1.59.value: Teamed interface failovers indicate critical network path failures affecting server connectivity.

1.1.60.title: MTU Mismatch Detection
1.1.60.value: MTU mismatches cause fragmentation and performance issues, especially with jumbo frame configurations.

1.1.61.title: TCP TIME_WAIT Accumulation
1.1.61.value: Excessive TIME_WAIT sockets can exhaust ephemeral port space, causing connection failures under load.

1.1.62.title: Network Bandwidth Utilization by Interface
1.1.62.value: High bandwidth utilization indicates potential capacity constraints or unexpected traffic patterns.

1.1.63.title: Dropped Packets by Network Interface
1.1.63.value: Dropped packets indicate network issues, buffer overflow, or driver problems affecting reliability.

1.1.64.title: Network Latency Monitoring (Ping RTT)
1.1.64.value: Elevated latency to critical services impacts application performance and user experience.

1.1.65.title: Auditd Rule Violation Detection
1.1.65.value: Auditd violations provide forensic evidence of security incidents and unauthorized system access.

1.1.66.title: SELinux Denial Monitoring
1.1.66.value: SELinux denials indicate policy violations that may require tuning or signal legitimate attack attempts.

1.1.67.title: AppArmor Profile Violation Detection
1.1.67.value: AppArmor violations indicate policy breaches that may reflect policy misconfigurations or attack attempts.

1.1.68.title: Rootkit Detection via File Integrity
1.1.68.value: File integrity changes indicate potential rootkit installation or unauthorized system modification.

1.1.69.title: SUID/SGID Binary Changes
1.1.69.value: Unauthorized SUID/SGID binary modifications enable privilege escalation attacks.

1.1.70.title: /etc/passwd Modifications
1.1.70.value: /etc/passwd changes indicate user account creation/modification requiring immediate investigation for unauthorized access.

1.1.71.title: /etc/shadow Modifications
1.1.71.value: /etc/shadow changes indicate password hash tampering or unauthorized privilege escalation attempts.

1.1.72.title: SSH Public Key Changes
1.1.72.value: SSH key modifications enable persistent unauthorized access, indicating potential account compromise.

1.1.73.title: PAM Authentication Failure Tracking
1.1.73.value: PAM failures indicate authentication issues or brute-force attack attempts against user accounts.

1.1.74.title: Login from Unusual Source IPs
1.1.74.value: Logins from unusual source IPs may indicate account compromise or unauthorized access.

1.1.75.title: Failed su Attempts
1.1.75.value: Failed su attempts indicate potential privilege escalation attempts or credential compromise.

1.1.76.title: Privilege Escalation Detection
1.1.76.value: Privilege escalation indicates successful security breach enabling attacker to gain administrative access.

1.1.77.title: Unauthorized Cron Job Additions
1.1.77.value: Unauthorized cron jobs enable persistent malware execution and data exfiltration.

1.1.78.title: Open Port Changes
1.1.78.value: New listening ports indicate service configuration changes or malware opening backdoors.

1.1.79.title: Setcap Binary Monitoring
1.1.79.value: Setcap binary modifications enable privilege escalation bypassing traditional privilege boundary enforcement.

1.1.80.title: Systemd Unit Failures
1.1.80.value: Service unit failures indicate application issues or configuration problems requiring immediate remediation.

1.1.81.title: Systemd Timer Missed Triggers
1.1.81.value: Missed systemd timers indicate scheduling issues or system overload preventing scheduled tasks.

1.1.82.title: D-State (Uninterruptible Sleep) Process Detection
1.1.82.value: D-state processes indicate hanging I/O operations or kernel deadlocks requiring immediate investigation.

1.1.83.title: Process CPU Affinity Changes
1.1.83.value: CPU affinity changes can indicate attempted performance optimization or malicious CPU isolation attempts.

1.1.84.title: Runaway Process Detection (CPU Hog)
1.1.84.value: Runaway processes consuming excessive CPU degrade performance for all workloads on the host.

1.1.85.title: Memory Hog Detection
1.1.85.value: Memory-consuming processes can cause OOM conditions affecting all applications on the host.

1.1.86.title: Fork Bomb Detection
1.1.86.value: Fork bombs exhaust PID space and system resources, making systems unusable.

1.1.87.title: Process Namespace Breakout Detection
1.1.87.value: Process namespace breakout indicates container escape or privilege escalation enabling access to host.

1.1.88.title: Container Escape Attempt Detection
1.1.88.value: Container escape attempts are critical security events indicating sophisticated attack against containerized infrastructure.

1.1.89.title: Syslog Flood Detection
1.1.89.value: Syslog floods can overwhelm log infrastructure and mask real security events in log noise.

1.1.90.title: Journal Disk Usage Monitoring
1.1.90.value: Journal disk usage growth can consume valuable storage space, potentially filling disks.

1.1.91.title: Log Rotation Failures
1.1.91.value: Log rotation failures can cause log files to grow unbounded, consuming disk space and impacting log analysis.

1.1.92.title: Auditd Daemon Health
1.1.92.value: Auditd daemon failure results in loss of security audit trail, creating compliance and forensic gaps.

1.1.93.title: Rsyslog Queue Backlog Monitoring
1.1.93.value: Rsyslog queue backlog indicates log forwarding issues or overload of remote syslog infrastructure.

1.1.94.title: Failed Log Forwarding
1.1.94.value: Failed log forwarding creates data loss in centralized logging infrastructure, creating gaps in monitoring and compliance.

1.1.95.title: TCP Connection Establishment Rate
1.1.95.value: High connection establishment rate may indicate application behavior changes or DDoS attack preparation.

1.1.96.title: NUMA Hit/Miss Ratio Tracking
1.1.96.value: Tracking NUMA hit/miss ratio identifies opportunities for workload optimization on NUMA systems.

1.1.97.title: CPU C-State Residency Monitoring
1.1.97.value: CPU C-state residency tracking optimizes power consumption and identifies power management issues.

1.1.98.title: TLB Shootdown Rate Monitoring
1.1.98.value: High TLB shootdown rates indicate excessive cross-CPU cache invalidations affecting performance on multi-CPU systems.

1.1.99.title: Kernel Lock Contention Detection
1.1.99.value: Kernel lock contention degrades multi-core scalability and application throughput.

1.1.100.title: Softirq Rate Monitoring
1.1.100.value: High softirq rates indicate kernel workload distribution issues or network stack pressure.

1.1.101.title: Context Switch Anomalies Detection
1.1.101.value: Context switch anomalies indicate scheduler issues or unexpected process workload changes.

1.1.102.title: EDAC Memory Error Tracking
1.1.102.value: EDAC memory errors indicate hardware failures predicting imminent memory or system failure.

1.1.103.title: IPMI Sensor Threshold Violations
1.1.103.value: IPMI sensor violations indicate hardware conditions (thermal, voltage, power) requiring immediate remediation.

1.1.104.title: Thermal Throttling Detection
1.1.104.value: Thermal throttling reduces CPU performance to prevent overheating, indicating cooling system issues.

1.1.105.title: Fan Speed Anomalies
1.1.105.value: Fan speed anomalies indicate cooling system degradation potentially leading to thermal overload.

1.1.106.title: Power Supply State Changes
1.1.106.value: Power supply state changes indicate hardware failures requiring immediate physical intervention.

1.1.107.title: Hardware Clock Drift Detection
1.1.107.value: Hardware clock drift affects system time accuracy impacting application consistency and audit trails.

1.1.108.title: Password Policy Violation Detection
1.1.108.value: Password policy violations indicate accounts with weak credentials vulnerable to compromise.

1.1.109.title: Account Expiry Monitoring
1.1.109.value: Account expiry tracking ensures user accounts remain valid and prevents access with expired credentials.

1.1.110.title: Inactive User Detection
1.1.110.value: Inactive users with enabled accounts represent security risk and should be disabled to reduce attack surface.

1.1.111.title: World-Writable File Detection
1.1.111.value: World-writable files can be modified by any user enabling privilege escalation or system compromise.

1.1.112.title: Unowned File Detection
1.1.112.value: Unowned files indicate potential file system corruption or security issue requiring investigation.

1.1.113.title: SETUID Audit and Tracking
1.1.113.value: SETUID binary execution enables privilege escalation requiring tracking for security monitoring.

1.1.114.title: Open File Handle Per-Process Monitoring
1.1.114.value: High open file handle counts per process can exhaust system limits causing application failures.

1.1.115.title: Listening Port Compliance
1.1.115.value: Port compliance ensures only authorized services are listening, reducing attack surface.

1.1.116.title: Installed Package Drift Detection
1.1.116.value: Package drift indicates unauthorized software installation or configuration management failures.

1.1.117.title: Configuration File Change Tracking (/etc)
1.1.117.value: Configuration file changes can indicate system compromise or unauthorized configuration modifications.

1.1.118.title: System Reboot Frequency Anomaly
1.1.118.value: Unexpected reboot frequency indicates system instability, crashes, or possible security incident response.

1.1.119.title: Defunct (Zombie) Process Accumulation
1.1.119.value: Accumulating zombie processes indicate application resource leaks causing process table exhaustion risk.

1.1.120.title: Symbolic Link Chain Depth Monitoring
1.1.120.value: Excessive symbolic link chains can cause performance issues and may indicate directory traversal vulnerabilities.

1.1.121.title: Bootloader Configuration Changes
1.1.121.value: Bootloader changes can enable persistence mechanisms or bypass security controls at boot time.

1.2.1.title: CPU Utilization Trending
1.2.1.value: Sustained high CPU causes application timeouts and service degradation. Trending enables capacity planning and helps identify runaway processes.

1.2.2.title: Memory Utilization & Paging
1.2.2.value: High memory and excessive paging degrade performance. Page file usage indicates the system is under memory pressure.

1.2.3.title: Disk Space Monitoring
1.2.3.value: Full disks crash applications, stop logging, and corrupt databases. Windows can become unbootable if the system drive fills.

1.2.4.title: Windows Service Failures
1.2.4.value: Stopped critical services directly impact application availability. Auto-restart doesn't always work, and some services can't auto-restart.

1.2.5.title: Event Log Flood Detection
1.2.5.value: Abnormal event log volumes often indicate error loops, misconfiguration, or an active attack. Also protects Splunk license from unexpected spikes.

1.2.6.title: Failed Login Monitoring
1.2.6.value: Detects credential stuffing, brute-force attacks, and compromised account usage. Key for security monitoring and compliance.

1.2.7.title: Account Lockout Tracking
1.2.7.value: Lockouts frustrate users and can indicate active attacks. Identifying the source computer of the lockout dramatically speeds resolution.

1.2.8.title: Privileged Group Changes
1.2.8.value: Additions to Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, or Schema Admins grant extreme privilege. Unauthorized changes could mean full domain compromise.

1.2.9.title: Windows Update Compliance
1.2.9.value: Unpatched systems are primary attack vectors. Tracking patch compliance across the fleet supports vulnerability management and regulatory requirements.

1.2.10.title: Scheduled Task Failures
1.2.10.value: Failed scheduled tasks break batch jobs, cleanup scripts, and automated processes. Often goes unnoticed until downstream effects appear.

1.2.11.title: Blue Screen of Death (BSOD)
1.2.11.value: BSODs indicate severe system instability — driver bugs, hardware failure, or memory corruption. Repeated BSODs on the same host demand immediate attention.

1.2.12.title: RDP Session Monitoring
1.2.12.value: Tracks who connected via Remote Desktop, from where, and when. Essential for compliance auditing and detecting lateral movement.

1.2.13.title: PowerShell Script Execution
1.2.13.value: PowerShell is the most common tool in modern Windows attacks (Cobalt Strike, Empire, fileless malware). Script block logging captures the actual code executed.

1.2.14.title: IIS Web Server Monitoring
1.2.14.value: IIS access logs provide visibility into web application health — error rates, response times, and request volumes. Critical for web-facing services.

1.2.15.title: DNS Server Health
1.2.15.value: DNS is foundational infrastructure — when DNS is slow or failing, everything fails. Monitoring query rates and failures ensures resolution reliability.

1.2.16.title: DHCP Scope Exhaustion
1.2.16.value: When DHCP scopes run out of addresses, new devices can't get network access. Often manifests as "network down" complaints.

1.2.17.title: Certificate Expiration
1.2.17.value: Expired certificates cause TLS failures, broken websites, authentication failures, and service outages. Among the most preventable outage causes.

1.2.18.title: Active Directory Replication
1.2.18.value: AD replication failures cause authentication inconsistencies — users locked out in one site but not another, stale GPOs, and split-brain scenarios.

1.2.19.title: Group Policy Processing Failures
1.2.19.value: GPO failures mean security policies, drive mappings, software deployments, and configurations aren't being applied. Systems may be running with stale or missing policies.

1.2.20.title: Print Spooler Issues
1.2.20.value: Print spooler crashes affect print services and have historically been attack vectors (PrintNightmare). Monitoring catches both operational and security issues.

1.2.21.title: Disk I/O Queue Length
1.2.21.value: Sustained high disk queue lengths indicate storage bottlenecks invisible to CPU/memory monitoring. Causes application hangs and timeout errors.

1.2.22.title: Process Handle Leak Detection
1.2.22.value: Handle leaks cause resource exhaustion and eventual application crashes or system instability. Detecting the leak early prevents unplanned outages.

1.2.23.title: Non-Paged Pool Exhaustion
1.2.23.value: Non-paged pool memory is limited kernel memory. Exhaustion causes BSOD (DRIVER_IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL). Often caused by driver leaks.

1.2.24.title: Network Interface Utilization
1.2.24.value: Saturated network interfaces cause packet drops, retransmissions, and application timeouts. Often missed when only CPU/memory are monitored.

1.2.25.title: Processor Queue Length
1.2.25.value: Processor queue length >2 per core indicates threads waiting for CPU time. Detects CPU contention even when average utilization looks normal due to burst patterns.

1.2.26.title: Security Log Cleared
1.2.26.value: Clearing the Security event log is a classic attacker technique to cover tracks. Legitimate clears are rare and should always be investigated.

1.2.27.title: New Service Installation
1.2.27.value: Attackers install malicious services for persistence. Unexpected service installations outside change windows indicate compromise or unauthorized software.

1.2.28.title: Windows Firewall Rule Changes
1.2.28.value: Unauthorized firewall rule changes can open attack vectors. Malware often disables the firewall or adds allow rules for C2 communication.

1.2.29.title: Registry Run Key Modification (Persistence)
1.2.29.value: Run/RunOnce registry keys are the most common malware persistence mechanism. Monitoring these keys catches many threats early.

1.2.30.title: LSASS Memory Access (Credential Dumping)
1.2.30.value: Accessing LSASS process memory is the primary technique for credential theft (Mimikatz, ProcDump). Detection is critical to stopping lateral movement.

1.2.31.title: Kerberos Authentication Failures
1.2.31.value: Kerberos failures (EventCode 4771) reveal password spraying, expired accounts, clock skew, and misconfigured SPNs. Distinct from NTLM failures and requires separate monitoring.

1.2.32.title: WMI Event Subscription Persistence
1.2.32.value: WMI event subscriptions are a stealthy persistence mechanism that survives reboots. Used by APT groups and fileless malware.

1.2.33.title: Audit Policy Changes
1.2.33.value: Attackers modify audit policies to disable logging and hide their activities. Any unauthorized audit policy change must be investigated immediately.

1.2.34.title: AppLocker / WDAC Policy Violations
1.2.34.value: AppLocker/WDAC blocks track unauthorized application execution attempts. High violation rates indicate persistent threats or misconfigured policies.

1.2.35.title: Windows Defender Threat Detections
1.2.35.value: Real-time visibility into endpoint AV detections across the fleet. Delayed response to malware detections increases blast radius.

1.2.36.title: DCSync Attack Detection
1.2.36.value: DCSync uses Directory Replication Service permissions to extract password hashes remotely. Detecting non-DC replication requests catches this attack before credential theft completes.

1.2.37.title: Kerberoasting Detection (SPN Ticket Requests)
1.2.37.value: Kerberoasting requests TGS tickets for service accounts with SPNs, then cracks them offline. Detecting anomalous TGS requests catches this before passwords are compromised.

1.2.38.title: AD Object Deletion Monitoring
1.2.38.value: Accidental or malicious deletion of AD objects (OUs, users, groups, computer accounts) can cause widespread service disruption. AD Recycle Bin has a limited window.

1.2.39.title: Domain Trust Changes
1.2.39.value: Unauthorized trust relationships can grant external domains access to internal resources. Trust modifications are rare and high-impact.

1.2.40.title: WHEA Hardware Error Reporting
1.2.40.value: Windows Hardware Error Architecture (WHEA) reports CPU, memory, and PCIe hardware errors before they cause crashes. Enables proactive hardware replacement.

1.2.41.title: Volume Shadow Copy Failures
1.2.41.value: VSS failures break backup chains, System Restore, and SQL/Exchange application-consistent snapshots. Often silent until a restore is attempted.

1.2.42.title: .NET CLR Performance Monitoring
1.2.42.value: .NET garbage collection pauses and high exception rates cause application latency and instability. CLR monitoring reveals issues invisible to external health checks.

1.2.43.title: Failover Cluster Event Monitoring
1.2.43.value: Cluster failovers indicate node failures or network partitions affecting high-availability services. Each failover risks brief downtime and potential data loss.

1.2.44.title: SMB Share Access Anomalies
1.2.44.value: Anomalous SMB share access patterns indicate lateral movement, data exfiltration, or ransomware file encryption across network shares.

1.2.45.title: Windows Time Service (W32Time) Issues
1.2.45.value: Time synchronization failures break Kerberos authentication (5-minute tolerance), cause log correlation issues, and invalidate audit trails.

1.2.46.title: DFS-R Replication Backlog
1.2.46.value: DFS-R replication backlogs mean file servers are out of sync. Users may access stale data, and a prolonged backlog can trigger an initial sync (full re-replication).

1.2.47.title: Application Crash (WER) Trending
1.2.47.value: Windows Error Reporting captures crash details for all applications. Trending reveals systemic instability, bad patches, or problematic application versions.

1.2.48.title: PowerShell Script Block Logging
1.2.48.value: Script Block Logging captures the full text of every PowerShell script executed, including deobfuscated code. Essential for detecting fileless attacks and encoded commands.

1.2.49.title: Lateral Movement via Explicit Credentials
1.2.49.value: Logon type 9 (NewCredentials / RunAs /netonly) and type 10 (RDP) from unexpected sources reveal credential abuse and lateral movement between systems.

1.2.50.title: DNS Debug Query Logging
1.2.50.value: DNS query logging reveals C2 communication via DNS tunneling, DGA domains, and unauthorized DNS resolution. Essential for security visibility.

1.2.51.title: Process Creation with Command Line Auditing
1.2.51.value: Full command-line visibility on process creation is the foundation of threat detection. Reveals encoded PowerShell, LOLBin abuse, and suspicious child processes.

1.2.52.title: NIC Teaming / LBFO Failover
1.2.52.value: NIC team member failures reduce redundancy silently. A second failure causes full network loss. Detecting the first failure enables proactive repair.

1.2.53.title: BitLocker Recovery Events
1.2.53.value: BitLocker recovery mode triggers indicate TPM issues, boot configuration changes, or potential tampering with the boot chain. Each event requires investigation.

1.2.54.title: Windows Event Forwarding (WEF) Health
1.2.54.value: WEF collects events from thousands of endpoints to central collectors. Forwarding failures create visibility gaps across the security monitoring pipeline.

1.2.55.title: Suspicious Token Manipulation
1.2.55.value: Token impersonation and privilege escalation via token manipulation (SeImpersonatePrivilege abuse) is a common post-exploitation technique.

1.2.56.title: Sysmon Network Connection Monitoring
1.2.56.value: Sysmon EventCode 3 logs every outbound TCP/UDP connection with the originating process. Reveals C2 callbacks, data exfiltration, and unauthorized network access.

1.2.57.title: Thread Count Exhaustion
1.2.57.value: Thread leaks or excessive thread creation cause pool exhaustion and application hangs. Windows has a system-wide limit of ~65K threads that affects all processes.

1.2.58.title: Storage Spaces Health Monitoring
1.2.58.value: Storage Spaces pools degrade silently when physical disks fail. Detection before a second disk fails prevents data loss in mirrored/parity configurations.

1.2.59.title: DCOM / COM+ Application Errors
1.2.59.value: DCOM errors affect distributed applications, WMI remote management, and MMC snap-ins. Persistent errors indicate permission issues or component registration corruption.

1.2.60.title: Code Integrity / Driver Signing Violations
1.2.60.value: Unsigned or tampered drivers loading into the kernel are a rootkit indicator. Code Integrity violations detect bypass attempts and driver-level threats.

1.2.61.title: Data Deduplication Health
1.2.61.value: Windows Data Deduplication saves significant storage on file servers. Job failures or savings degradation indicate volume corruption or configuration issues.

1.2.62.title: TCP Connection State Monitoring
1.2.62.value: Excessive TIME_WAIT, CLOSE_WAIT, or ESTABLISHED connections indicate connection leaks, exhausted ephemeral ports, or application hanging. Causes service unavailability.

1.2.63.title: Windows Installer Failures
1.2.63.value: MSI installation failures affect patching, software deployment, and SCCM/Intune compliance. Repeated failures indicate corrupted Windows Installer service or disk issues.

1.2.64.title: Event Log Channel Size / Overflow
1.2.64.value: When event logs reach maximum size with overwrite-oldest policy, critical security events are lost. With do-not-overwrite policy, the log stops recording entirely.

1.2.65.title: Pass-the-Hash / NTLM Relay Detection
1.2.65.value: Pass-the-hash attacks use stolen NTLM hashes to authenticate without knowing the password. Detecting NTLM logons from unusual sources catches this common lateral movement technique.

1.2.66.title: Sysmon File Creation in Suspicious Paths
1.2.66.value: Files created in temp directories, startup folders, and system paths by unexpected processes indicate malware dropping payloads or establishing persistence.

1.2.67.title: Golden Ticket Detection (TGT Anomalies)
1.2.67.value: Golden tickets are forged Kerberos TGTs that grant domain-wide access. Detecting anomalous TGT properties catches this catastrophic compromise.

1.2.68.title: NTFS Corruption and Self-Healing
1.2.68.value: NTFS corruption can cause data loss, application failures, and boot issues. Self-healing events indicate disk degradation that will worsen.

1.2.69.title: Page File Usage & Exhaustion
1.2.69.value: Page file exhaustion prevents new process creation and causes "out of virtual memory" errors. System-managed page files can grow to fill the disk.

1.2.70.title: Context Switch Rate Anomalies
1.2.70.value: Abnormally high context switch rates indicate excessive threading, poor application design, or kernel-mode driver issues. Degrades overall system performance.

1.2.71.title: Scheduled Task Creation (Persistence)
1.2.71.value: Scheduled tasks are a common persistence mechanism for malware. New tasks created outside change management warrant investigation.

1.2.72.title: WinRM / Remote PowerShell Connections
1.2.72.value: WinRM enables remote command execution via PowerShell Remoting. Monitoring inbound WinRM sessions detects lateral movement and unauthorized remote management.

1.2.73.title: LDAP Query Performance (DC Health)
1.2.73.value: Slow LDAP queries on domain controllers degrade authentication, group policy processing, and application lookups across the entire domain.

1.2.74.title: Hyper-V VM State Changes
1.2.74.value: Unexpected VM power state changes (shutdowns, paused, critical saves) indicate host issues, resource contention, or unauthorized administrative actions.

1.2.75.title: AD Certificate Services (ADCS) Anomalies
1.2.75.value: ADCS misconfigurations enable privilege escalation (ESC1-ESC8 attacks). Monitoring certificate requests catches unauthorized certificate enrollment for domain admin impersonation.

1.2.76.title: AdminSDHolder Modification
1.2.76.value: The AdminSDHolder container controls ACLs on all privileged AD groups. Modifying it grants persistent hidden admin access that survives permission resets.

1.2.77.title: SPN Modification (Targeted Kerberoasting)
1.2.77.value: Attackers add SPNs to admin accounts to make them Kerberoastable. Monitoring SPN changes on sensitive accounts catches this setup before the actual attack.

1.2.78.title: DSRM Account Usage
1.2.78.value: The Directory Services Restore Mode (DSRM) account is a local admin on every DC with a rarely-changed password. Its use outside restores indicates compromise.

1.2.79.title: Sysmon DNS Query Logging
1.2.79.value: Per-process DNS query logging reveals which applications communicate with which domains. Detects DGA, C2 callbacks, and data exfiltration at the endpoint level.

1.2.80.title: Windows Backup Job Monitoring
1.2.80.value: Windows Server Backup failures mean the server has no recovery point. Silent failures create a false sense of protection.

1.2.81.title: SMBv1 Usage Detection
1.2.81.value: SMBv1 is vulnerable to EternalBlue and WannaCry. Detecting remaining SMBv1 traffic identifies systems that need upgrading or have SMBv1 re-enabled.

1.2.82.title: Credential Guard Status Monitoring
1.2.82.value: Credential Guard protects NTLM hashes and Kerberos tickets in an isolated container. Monitoring ensures it remains enabled and isn't bypassed.

1.2.83.title: Boot Configuration Changes (BCDEdit)
1.2.83.value: Boot configuration changes can disable Secure Boot, enable test signing (rootkit loading), or modify boot chain integrity. Used by advanced threats.

1.2.84.title: Sysmon Named Pipe Monitoring
1.2.84.value: Named pipes are used for inter-process communication and by tools like Cobalt Strike, Mimikatz, and PsExec. Detecting unusual named pipes reveals C2 and lateral movement.

1.2.85.title: IIS Application Pool Crashes & Recycling
1.2.85.value: Application pool crashes cause HTTP 503 errors and service outages. Frequent recycling indicates memory leaks or configuration issues in web applications.

1.2.86.title: NTLM Audit and Restriction Monitoring
1.2.86.value: NTLM is a legacy authentication protocol vulnerable to relay attacks. Auditing NTLM usage identifies applications and systems that need migration to Kerberos.

1.2.87.title: DPAPI Credential Backup (DC)
1.2.87.value: Data Protection API master key backup to domain controllers enables credential theft. Abnormal DPAPI backup activity from unexpected accounts indicates compromise.

1.2.88.title: Windows Search Indexer Issues
1.2.88.value: Search Indexer crashes and high resource usage affect file server performance and SharePoint crawling. Index corruption requires full rebuild.

1.2.89.title: System Uptime & Unexpected Restarts
1.2.89.value: Unexpected restarts indicate BSOD, power loss, forced reboots, or patch installations. Tracking uptime reveals instability patterns and unauthorized maintenance.

1.2.90.title: Shadow Copy Deletion (Ransomware Indicator)
1.2.90.value: Ransomware deletes volume shadow copies to prevent file recovery. Detecting vssadmin/wmic shadow deletion commands is a high-confidence ransomware indicator.

1.2.91.title: USB / Removable Device Auditing
1.2.91.value: Removable storage devices are a data exfiltration vector. Auditing device connections enables DLP and compliance enforcement.

1.2.92.title: Remote Desktop Gateway Session Monitoring
1.2.92.value: RD Gateway is the entry point for remote workers. Monitoring session lifecycle detects unauthorized access, session hijacking, and resource abuse.

1.2.93.title: Group Policy Object (GPO) Modification Auditing
1.2.93.value: GPO changes affect all domain-joined systems. Unauthorized GPO modifications can deploy malware, weaken security, or exfiltrate credentials at scale.

1.2.94.title: Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) Activity
1.2.94.value: WSL can be abused to run Linux-based attack tools while evading Windows-focused security tooling. Monitoring WSL activity closes this visibility gap.

1.2.95.title: Windows Container Health Monitoring
1.2.95.value: Windows containers running on Server 2019+ need monitoring for resource limits, failures, and networking issues to ensure application availability.

1.2.96.title: DNS Server Zone Transfer Monitoring
1.2.96.value: Zone transfers expose the entire DNS namespace to attackers. Unauthorized zone transfers enable reconnaissance and must be detected immediately.

1.2.97.title: Print Spooler Vulnerability Monitoring (PrintNightmare)
1.2.97.value: Print Spooler vulnerabilities (CVE-2021-34527, CVE-2021-1675) enable remote code execution and privilege escalation. Continuous monitoring ensures patches hold and exploitation attempts are caught.

1.2.98.title: NPS / RADIUS Authentication Monitoring
1.2.98.value: Network Policy Server handles VPN, Wi-Fi, and 802.1X authentication. Monitoring NPS detects brute-force attacks, misconfigured policies, and unauthorized network access.

1.2.99.title: MSMQ Queue Depth Monitoring
1.2.99.value: Message queue buildup indicates application processing failures. Monitoring queue depth prevents message loss and detects downstream system outages.

1.2.100.title: PKI / Certificate Authority Health
1.2.100.value: An enterprise CA issues certificates for authentication, encryption, and code signing. CA failures break SSO, VPN, Wi-Fi, and TLS across the organization.

1.2.101.title: File Share Access Auditing (SMB)
1.2.101.value: File share access auditing detects unauthorized data access, lateral movement via mapped drives, and ransomware encrypting network shares.

1.2.102.title: Software Restriction / AppLocker Bypass Detection
1.2.102.value: Application whitelisting is a primary defense against malware. Detecting bypass attempts reveals both sophisticated attackers and policy gaps.

1.2.103.title: Terminal Services / RDP Session Tracking
1.2.103.value: RDP is a primary lateral movement vector. Complete session tracking from logon to logoff enables detection of compromised credentials and unauthorized access.

1.2.104.title: Disk Latency and I/O Performance
1.2.104.value: High disk latency directly impacts application performance and user experience. Proactive monitoring prevents performance degradation and identifies failing storage.

1.2.105.title: Windows Defender Exclusion Monitoring
1.2.105.value: Attackers add Defender exclusions to hide malware. Monitoring exclusion changes detects evasion techniques and ensures antivirus coverage remains complete.

1.2.106.title: Local Administrator Group Membership Changes
1.2.106.value: Local admin privileges enable credential theft, persistence, and lateral movement. Monitoring local admin group changes detects privilege escalation attacks.

1.2.107.title: DFS Replication Health Monitoring
1.2.107.value: DFS-R synchronizes SYSVOL and shared folders across domain controllers and file servers. Replication failures cause inconsistent GPOs and stale data.

1.2.108.title: Kerberos Constrained Delegation Abuse
1.2.108.value: Kerberos delegation allows services to impersonate users. Misconfigured or compromised delegation targets enable privilege escalation to domain admin.

1.2.109.title: Windows Time Service (W32Time) Drift
1.2.109.value: Kerberos authentication fails when clock skew exceeds 5 minutes. Time drift breaks authentication, log correlation, and forensic timelines.

1.2.110.title: PowerShell Constrained Language Mode Bypass
1.2.110.value: Constrained Language Mode limits PowerShell attack surface. Detecting bypasses reveals attackers escalating from restricted to full-language mode for malware execution.

1.2.111.title: Windows Firewall Rule Tampering
1.2.111.value: Attackers disable or modify firewall rules to enable lateral movement, C2 communication, and data exfiltration. Rule changes outside maintenance windows indicate compromise.

1.2.112.title: BITS Transfer Abuse Detection
1.2.112.value: Background Intelligent Transfer Service (BITS) is abused by malware for stealthy downloads and persistence. Monitoring BITS jobs detects LOLBin-based attacks.

1.2.113.title: COM Object Hijacking Detection
1.2.113.value: COM hijacking replaces legitimate COM objects with malicious ones for persistence and privilege escalation. It's a stealthy technique that survives reboots.

1.2.114.title: LSASS Memory Protection Monitoring
1.2.114.value: LSASS contains credentials in memory. Monitoring LSASS access attempts and protection status detects credential dumping tools like Mimikatz.

1.2.115.title: Logon Session Anomalies (Type 3 / Network Logon)
1.2.115.value: Network logons (Type 3) from unexpected sources indicate lateral movement with stolen credentials. Baselining normal patterns reveals compromised accounts.

1.2.116.title: WMI Persistence Detection
1.2.116.value: WMI event subscriptions provide fileless persistence that survives reboots. Detecting WMI persistence reveals advanced persistent threats.

1.2.117.title: NIC Teaming & Network Adapter Failures
1.2.117.value: NIC teaming provides network redundancy for servers. Adapter failures reduce redundancy and can cause outages if the remaining NIC also fails.

1.2.118.title: ASR (Attack Surface Reduction) Rule Monitoring
1.2.118.value: ASR rules block common attack techniques (Office macro code, credential theft, ransomware). Monitoring ASR ensures rules are enforced and detects blocked attacks.

1.2.119.title: Registry Run Key Persistence Monitoring
1.2.119.value: Registry Run keys are the most common persistence mechanism for malware. Monitoring autostart registry locations detects new malware installations.

1.2.120.title: BitLocker Recovery & Compliance Monitoring
1.2.120.value: BitLocker protects data at rest. Monitoring recovery events detects unauthorized hardware changes, and compliance tracking ensures all endpoints are encrypted.

1.2.121.title: DNS Client Query Anomalies
1.2.121.value: Monitoring DNS queries from Windows clients reveals C2 beacons, DNS tunneling, and DGA-based malware communicating with attacker infrastructure.

1.2.122.title: Local Account Creation & Modification
1.2.122.value: Creating local accounts is a persistence technique. On domain-joined systems, local account creation is rare and suspicious.

1.2.123.title: Token Manipulation / Privilege Escalation
1.2.123.value: Token manipulation (impersonation, token duplication) allows attackers to escalate privileges. Detecting abuse of SeImpersonatePrivilege catches potato-style attacks.

1.2.124.title: Process Injection Detection (Sysmon)
1.2.124.value: Process injection hides malicious code inside legitimate processes. Detecting injection techniques (CreateRemoteThread, APC, process hollowing) catches advanced malware.

1.2.125.title: Cluster Shared Volume (CSV) Health
1.2.125.value: Cluster Shared Volumes underpin Hyper-V and SQL Server failover clusters. CSV failures cause VM/database unavailability across the cluster.

1.2.126.title: DCOM Activation Failures
1.2.126.value: DCOM failures break distributed applications, WMI remote management, and SCCM client operations. Monitoring identifies misconfigured permissions and network issues.

1.2.127.title: Automatic Windows Update Compliance
1.2.127.value: Unpatched systems are the primary attack surface. Tracking Windows Update status across all systems ensures timely patching and compliance reporting.

1.2.128.title: Service Account Logon Anomalies
1.2.128.value: Compromised service accounts grant persistent access and often have elevated privileges. Detecting anomalous service account behavior catches credential theft early.

1.2.129.title: Sysmon Driver/Image Load Monitoring
1.2.129.value: Monitoring driver and DLL loads catches rootkits, vulnerable drivers, and DLL side-loading attacks that evade process-level monitoring.

1.2.130.title: Scheduled Task Modification for Persistence
1.2.130.value: Modifying existing scheduled tasks is stealthier than creating new ones. Attackers replace legitimate task actions to achieve persistence without new artifacts.

1.3.1.title: System Resource Monitoring
1.3.1.value: Endpoint performance visibility helps IT support triage user complaints and identify machines needing replacement or upgrades.

1.3.2.title: FileVault Encryption Status
1.3.2.value: Unencrypted endpoints are a data breach risk if lost or stolen. Compliance requirement for most security frameworks (SOC2, ISO27001, PCI).

1.3.3.title: Gatekeeper and SIP Status
1.3.3.value: Disabled Gatekeeper or System Integrity Protection weakens macOS security posture. May indicate developer override or tampering.

1.3.4.title: Software Update Compliance
1.3.4.value: Unpatched macOS endpoints are vulnerable. Tracking update levels across the fleet supports vulnerability management.

1.3.5.title: Application Crash Monitoring
1.3.5.value: Frequent application crashes degrade user experience and may indicate malware, resource issues, or incompatible software.

1.4.1.title: Hardware Sensor Monitoring
1.4.1.value: Temperature, voltage, and fan speed anomalies predict impending hardware failures before they cause unplanned downtime.

1.4.2.title: RAID Degradation Alerts
1.4.2.value: A degraded RAID has lost redundancy — another disk failure means data loss. Requires immediate attention.

1.4.3.title: Power Supply Failure
1.4.3.value: Lost power supply redundancy means a single PSU failure away from an unplanned outage. Replacement needs to happen before the remaining PSU fails.

1.4.4.title: Predictive Disk Failure
1.4.4.value: SMART attributes can predict disk failure days or weeks in advance, enabling proactive replacement during maintenance windows.

1.4.5.title: Firmware Version Compliance
1.4.5.value: Outdated firmware may have security vulnerabilities or known bugs. Fleet-wide firmware tracking supports patch management.

1.4.6.title: Memory ECC Error Trending
1.4.6.value: Correctable ECC errors that increase over time strongly predict impending DIMM failure. Proactive replacement avoids unrecoverable memory errors and system crashes.

2.1.1.title: ESXi Host CPU Contention
2.1.1.value: CPU ready time measures how long a VM waits for physical CPU. High values (>5%) mean the host is overcommitted and VMs are starved for compute.

2.1.2.title: ESXi Host Memory Ballooning
2.1.2.value: Memory ballooning means the hypervisor is reclaiming memory from VMs. Swapping at the hypervisor level is worse — causes severe VM performance degradation invisible to the guest OS.

2.1.3.title: Datastore Capacity Trending
2.1.3.value: A full datastore prevents VM disk writes, causing crashes and corruption. Datastores fill gradually from VM growth, snapshots, and log accumulation.

2.1.4.title: Datastore Latency Spikes
2.1.4.value: Storage latency >20ms significantly impacts VM performance. Detects SAN issues, datastore contention, or storage path problems before applications are affected.

2.1.5.title: VM Snapshot Sprawl
2.1.5.value: Old snapshots consume datastore space exponentially, degrade VM I/O performance, and complicate backups. Snapshots >72 hours old are generally a problem.

2.1.6.title: vMotion Tracking
2.1.6.value: Tracks VM migrations for troubleshooting and change management. Excessive vMotion can indicate DRS instability or resource contention.

2.1.7.title: HA Failover Events
2.1.7.value: HA failover means a host failed and VMs were restarted on surviving hosts. Indicates hardware failure and potential capacity risk on remaining hosts.

2.1.8.title: DRS Imbalance Detection
2.1.8.value: DRS should keep clusters balanced. Frequent or failed DRS recommendations indicate resource constraints, affinity rule conflicts, or misconfiguration.

2.1.9.title: VM Sprawl Detection
2.1.9.value: Orphaned, powered-off, or idle VMs waste storage, IP addresses, backup capacity, and licenses. Regular cleanup frees significant resources.

2.1.10.title: vSAN Health Monitoring
2.1.10.value: vSAN is the storage fabric for many VMware clusters. Degraded vSAN health can cause VM data loss and cluster-wide outages.

2.1.11.title: ESXi Host Hardware Alerts
2.1.11.value: CIM-based hardware health detects physical component failures (fans, PSU, temperature) at the hypervisor level before they cause host failure.

2.1.12.title: VM Resource Over-Allocation
2.1.12.value: VMs consistently using <20% of allocated CPU/memory waste resources that other VMs could use. Right-sizing saves money and improves cluster capacity.

2.1.13.title: vCenter Alarm Correlation
2.1.13.value: Centralizing all vCenter alarms in Splunk enables correlation with other infrastructure data, historical trending, and unified alerting.

2.1.14.title: ESXi Patch Compliance
2.1.14.value: Unpatched ESXi hosts have known vulnerabilities. Fleet-wide version tracking ensures consistent patching and audit compliance.

2.1.15.title: VM Creation/Deletion Audit
2.1.15.value: Tracks VM lifecycle for change management compliance and resource governance. Detects unauthorized VM creation or suspicious deletions.

2.2.1.title: VM Performance Monitoring
2.2.1.value: Per-VM CPU, memory, and disk metrics identify resource contention and performance bottlenecks within the Hyper-V environment.

2.2.2.title: Hyper-V Replication Health
2.2.2.value: Replication lag means your DR site is behind. If replication breaks, you lose your recovery point objective (RPO).

2.2.3.title: Cluster Shared Volume Health
2.2.3.value: CSV issues can cause VM storage access failures across the entire cluster. Redirected I/O mode significantly degrades performance.

2.2.4.title: Live Migration Tracking
2.2.4.value: Audit trail for VM mobility. Excessive live migrations may indicate cluster imbalance or storage issues.

2.2.5.title: Integration Services Version
2.2.5.value: Outdated integration services cause performance issues and prevent features like time sync, heartbeat, and data exchange from working correctly.

2.3.1.title: Guest VM Resource Monitoring
2.3.1.value: Per-VM resource tracking for capacity planning and performance troubleshooting in KVM environments.

2.3.2.title: Host Overcommit Detection
2.3.2.value: Overcommitted KVM hosts cause all VMs to compete for resources. Unlike VMware, KVM doesn't have sophisticated DRS — manual balancing is needed.

2.3.3.title: VM Lifecycle Events
2.3.3.value: Audit trail for VM start, stop, migrate, and crash events. Essential for troubleshooting and change management in open-source virtualization.

3.1.1.title: Container Crash Loops
3.1.1.value: Containers restarting repeatedly indicate application bugs, misconfiguration, or dependency failures. Crash loops consume resources and never reach healthy state.

3.1.2.title: Container OOM Kills
3.1.2.value: OOM kills mean the container exceeded its memory limit. The application is either leaking memory or undersized. Data loss is likely.

3.1.3.title: Container CPU Throttling
3.1.3.value: CPU throttling means the container is hitting its CPU limit and being artificially slowed. Causes latency spikes invisible to standard CPU utilization metrics.

3.1.4.title: Container Memory Utilization
3.1.4.value: Tracking memory usage relative to limits catches containers approaching OOM before they're killed. Enables proactive limit adjustments.

3.1.5.title: Image Vulnerability Scanning
3.1.5.value: Container images with known CVEs are deployed directly into production. Scanning and tracking vulnerabilities prevents running exploitable workloads.

3.1.6.title: Privileged Container Detection
3.1.6.value: Privileged containers have full host access — a container escape gives root on the host. Should be flagged and justified in production.

3.1.7.title: Container Sprawl
3.1.7.value: Stopped containers and dangling images waste disk space. In development environments, sprawl can consume all available storage.

3.1.8.title: Docker Daemon Errors
3.1.8.value: Docker daemon errors affect all containers on the host. Network, storage driver, and containerd errors can cause widespread container failures.

3.2.1.title: Pod Restart Rate
3.2.1.value: High restart counts indicate application instability. Pods may appear "Running" but are constantly crashing and restarting, degrading service quality.

3.2.2.title: Pod Scheduling Failures
3.2.2.value: Pods stuck in Pending can't serve traffic. Usually caused by insufficient CPU/memory, node affinity rules, or persistent volume claim issues.

3.2.3.title: Node NotReady Detection
3.2.3.value: A NotReady node can't run pods. Existing pods are evicted after the toleration timeout (default 5 min). Causes service disruption if no replacement capacity.

3.2.4.title: Resource Quota Exhaustion
3.2.4.value: When namespace quotas are exhausted, new pods can't be created. Impacts deployments, autoscaling, and job scheduling within the namespace.

3.2.5.title: Persistent Volume Claims
3.2.5.value: Unbound PVCs prevent stateful workloads (databases, message queues) from starting. Often caused by storage class misconfiguration or capacity.

3.2.6.title: Deployment Rollout Failures
3.2.6.value: A failed rollout means new code isn't deploying successfully. Pods may be crash-looping, image pulls failing, or health checks not passing.

3.2.7.title: Control Plane Health
3.2.7.value: The control plane (API server, etcd, scheduler, controller-manager) is the brain of Kubernetes. Degradation affects all cluster operations.

3.2.8.title: etcd Cluster Health
3.2.8.value: etcd stores all Kubernetes state. etcd problems (leader elections, compaction failures, high latency) cascade into cluster-wide failures.

3.2.9.title: Ingress Error Rates
3.2.9.value: Ingress controllers are the front door to your services. High error rates mean users are getting errors. Catches backend failures and misconfigurations.

3.2.10.title: CrashLoopBackOff Detection
3.2.10.value: CrashLoopBackOff is the most common Kubernetes failure mode. The pod is crashing, restarting, and crashing again with exponential backoff. Service is down.

3.2.11.title: HPA Scaling Events
3.2.11.value: HPA scaling events show when applications are hitting capacity. Repeated max-scale events indicate undersized limits or unexpected traffic.

3.2.12.title: RBAC Audit
3.2.12.value: RBAC misconfigurations grant excessive permissions. Unauthorized access attempts indicate potential compromise or misconfigured service accounts.

3.2.13.title: Certificate Expiration
3.2.13.value: Kubernetes uses TLS certificates extensively (API server, kubelet, etcd). Expired certs cause cluster communication failures and outages.

3.2.14.title: Container Image Pull Failures
3.2.14.value: ImagePullBackOff prevents pods from starting. Caused by wrong image tags, registry auth failures, or network issues. Blocks deployments.

3.2.15.title: DaemonSet Completeness
3.2.15.value: DaemonSets (monitoring agents, log forwarders, network plugins) must run on every eligible node. Missing instances create monitoring or networking gaps.

3.3.1.title: Cluster Version & Upgrade Status
3.3.1.value: OpenShift upgrades can stall. Tracking upgrade progress and version across clusters ensures consistency and support compliance.

3.3.2.title: Operator Degraded Detection
3.3.2.value: Cluster operators manage core OpenShift components (networking, ingress, monitoring, authentication). Degraded operators mean partial cluster functionality loss.

3.3.3.title: Build Failure Monitoring
3.3.3.value: OpenShift Source-to-Image (S2I) build failures block application deployments. Trend analysis reveals systemic build infrastructure issues.

3.3.4.title: SCC Violation Detection
3.3.4.value: Security Context Constraint violations mean pods are attempting to run with permissions beyond their allowed scope. Could indicate misconfiguration or an attack.

3.4.1.title: Image Push/Pull Audit
3.4.1.value: Audit trail for who pushed or pulled what images. Detects unauthorized access, supply chain concerns, and usage patterns.

3.4.2.title: Vulnerability Scan Results
3.4.2.value: Registry-level scanning catches vulnerabilities before images are deployed. Trending shows whether security posture is improving or degrading.

3.4.3.title: Storage Quota Monitoring
3.4.3.value: Registry storage exhaustion prevents image pushes, blocking CI/CD pipelines. Monitoring enables proactive cleanup policy tuning.

4.1.1.title: Unauthorized API Calls
4.1.1.value: AccessDenied errors reveal reconnaissance activity, compromised credentials with insufficient permissions, or misconfigurations. Early indicator of attack or drift.

4.1.2.title: Root Account Usage
4.1.2.value: The AWS root account has unrestricted access and should never be used for daily operations. Any root activity is a critical security event.

4.1.3.title: Security Group Changes
4.1.3.value: Security group changes can expose services to the internet. Unauthorized modifications are a primary attack vector and compliance violation.

4.1.4.title: IAM Policy Changes
4.1.4.value: IAM policy changes affect who can do what across the entire AWS account. Unauthorized policy attachments can grant admin access.

4.1.5.title: Console Login Without MFA
4.1.5.value: Console access without MFA is a security risk — compromised passwords alone can grant full account access. Most compliance frameworks require MFA.

4.1.6.title: EC2 Instance State Changes
4.1.6.value: Tracks instance lifecycle for audit and change management. Unexpected terminations indicate accidents, auto-scaling issues, or attacks.

4.1.7.title: S3 Bucket Policy Changes
4.1.7.value: S3 bucket policy changes can expose sensitive data to the public internet. One of the most common cloud security incidents.

4.1.8.title: GuardDuty Finding Ingestion
4.1.8.value: GuardDuty provides ML-powered threat detection for AWS accounts. Centralizing findings in Splunk enables correlation with other security data.

4.1.9.title: VPC Flow Log Analysis
4.1.9.value: VPC Flow Logs provide network-level visibility into all traffic. Detects rejected traffic, data exfiltration, lateral movement, and network anomalies.

4.1.10.title: EC2 Performance Monitoring
4.1.10.value: CloudWatch metrics provide host-level performance data without agents. Baseline trending for capacity planning and anomaly detection.

4.1.11.title: RDS Performance Insights
4.1.11.value: Database performance issues directly impact application experience. Monitoring connections, CPU, IOPS, and replica lag catches problems before users notice.

4.1.12.title: Lambda Error Rate Monitoring
4.1.12.value: Lambda errors affect serverless application reliability. Timeouts indicate functions need more memory/time. Throttling means concurrency limits are hit.

4.1.13.title: EKS/ECS Cluster Health
4.1.13.value: Managed container orchestration health ensures application workloads are running correctly across the AWS compute fabric.

4.1.14.title: Cost Anomaly Detection
4.1.14.value: Unexpected spend spikes indicate runaway resources, cryptomining attacks, or misconfigured services. Catching anomalies early saves money.

4.1.15.title: Config Compliance Monitoring
4.1.15.value: AWS Config rules continuously evaluate resource compliance against security best practices. Non-compliant resources are attack surface.

4.1.16.title: KMS Key Usage Audit
4.1.16.value: Encryption key usage audit ensures data protection compliance. Unusual key access patterns may indicate unauthorized data decryption.

4.1.17.title: Elastic IP Association
4.1.17.value: Unassociated Elastic IPs cost money. Tracking associations supports inventory accuracy and cost management.

4.1.18.title: CloudFormation Stack Drift
4.1.18.value: Drift means infrastructure no longer matches its declared template — manual changes have been made. This breaks IaC and causes inconsistencies.

4.1.19.title: WAF Blocked Request Analysis
4.1.19.value: WAF blocks reveal attack patterns targeting your applications. Analysis helps tune rules and understand the threat landscape.

4.1.20.title: Reserved Instance Utilization
4.1.20.value: Underutilized RIs waste money. Tracking RI coverage and utilization helps optimize commit spending vs. on-demand costs.

4.2.1.title: Azure Activity Log Monitoring
4.2.1.value: Activity Log captures all control plane operations across Azure subscriptions. Essential audit trail for resource management and compliance.

4.2.2.title: Entra ID Sign-In Anomalies
4.2.2.value: Risky sign-ins include impossible travel, unfamiliar locations, and anonymous IP usage. Primary detection layer for account compromise.

4.2.3.title: Entra ID Privilege Escalation
4.2.3.value: Privileged role assignments (Global Admin, Privileged Role Admin) grant extreme power. Unauthorized assignments mean full tenant compromise.

4.2.4.title: NSG Flow Log Analysis
4.2.4.value: NSG Flow Logs provide Azure network-level visibility. Detects blocked traffic, anomalous patterns, and lateral movement within VNets.

4.2.5.title: Azure VM Performance
4.2.5.value: Azure Monitor metrics provide VM performance data without agents. Essential for capacity planning and correlating with application issues.

4.2.6.title: Azure SQL Performance
4.2.6.value: DTU/vCore exhaustion causes query throttling. Deadlocks and long-running queries impact application performance directly.

4.2.7.title: AKS Cluster Health
4.2.7.value: AKS cluster health monitoring ensures Kubernetes workloads are running reliably on Azure's managed platform.

4.2.8.title: Azure Key Vault Access Audit
4.2.8.value: Key Vault stores secrets, keys, and certificates. Unauthorized or anomalous access could indicate credential theft or data breach preparation.

4.2.9.title: Defender for Cloud Alerts
4.2.9.value: Microsoft Defender provides threat detection across Azure resources. Centralizing in Splunk enables cross-platform security correlation.

4.2.10.title: Storage Account Access Anomalies
4.2.10.value: Unusual storage access patterns may indicate data exfiltration or compromised service principals accessing sensitive data.

4.2.11.title: Resource Health Events
4.2.11.value: Azure service health impacts your resources directly. Knowing when Azure itself is having problems prevents wasted troubleshooting time.

4.2.12.title: Cost Management Alerts
4.2.12.value: Azure cost monitoring prevents budget overruns. Tracking spend by resource group/team enables chargeback and anomaly detection.

4.3.1.title: Audit Log Monitoring
4.3.1.value: GCP audit logs capture all admin activity and data access. Foundational for security monitoring and compliance in GCP environments.

4.3.2.title: IAM Policy Changes
4.3.2.value: IAM binding changes control who can access what in GCP. Unauthorized changes to bindings on projects, folders, or organizations are critical security events.

4.3.3.title: VPC Flow Log Analysis
4.3.3.value: GCP VPC Flow Logs provide network traffic visibility. Same use case as AWS/Azure — detect rejected traffic, anomalies, exfiltration.

4.3.4.title: GKE Cluster Health
4.3.4.value: GKE cluster health monitoring for managed Kubernetes in GCP. Node pools, upgrade status, and workload health.

4.3.5.title: Security Command Center
4.3.5.value: SCC provides vulnerability findings and threat detections across GCP. Centralizing in Splunk enables multi-cloud security correlation.

4.3.6.title: GCE Instance Monitoring
4.3.6.value: Compute Engine VM performance monitoring for capacity planning and baseline trending without guest-level agents.

4.3.7.title: BigQuery Audit and Cost
4.3.7.value: BigQuery can generate massive costs from poorly optimized queries. Audit and cost tracking prevents bill shock and identifies optimization opportunities.

4.3.8.title: Cloud Run/Functions Errors
4.3.8.value: Serverless function errors and cold starts impact application reliability and user experience.

4.4.1.title: Terraform Drift Detection
4.4.1.value: Infrastructure drift from declared IaC state means manual changes broke the single source of truth. Causes unpredictable behavior and deployment failures.

4.4.2.title: Cross-Cloud Identity Correlation
4.4.2.value: Users often have identities across AWS/Azure/GCP. Correlating activity provides unified view for security investigation and insider threat detection.

4.4.3.title: Multi-Cloud Cost Dashboard
4.4.3.value: Unified cost visibility across cloud providers enables budgeting, chargeback, and optimization decisions from a single pane of glass.

4.4.4.title: Cloud Resource Tagging Compliance
4.4.4.value: Untagged resources can't be tracked for cost allocation, compliance, or ownership. Tagging compliance is foundational for cloud governance.

5.1.1.title: Interface Up/Down Events
5.1.1.value: Link state changes directly impact connectivity. Flapping interfaces cause intermittent outages.

5.1.2.title: Interface Error Rates
5.1.2.value: CRC errors, drops indicate cabling, transceiver, or duplex issues.

5.1.3.title: Interface Utilization
5.1.3.value: Saturated links cause drops and congestion. Trending enables proactive upgrades.

5.1.4.title: BGP Peer State Changes
5.1.4.value: BGP session drops cause routing convergence, potentially making networks unreachable.

5.1.5.title: OSPF Neighbor Adjacency
5.1.5.value: OSPF neighbor loss triggers SPF recalculation, disrupting traffic.

5.1.6.title: Spanning Tree Topology Change
5.1.6.value: STP topology changes cause brief disruption and MAC flushing. Root bridge changes are critical.

5.1.7.title: Configuration Change Detection
5.1.7.value: Unauthorized config changes are a top cause of outages. Essential for compliance.

5.1.8.title: Device CPU/Memory Utilization
5.1.8.value: CPU exhaustion causes packet drops, routing failures, management unresponsiveness.

5.1.9.title: Device Uptime / Reload Tracking
5.1.9.value: Unexpected reboots indicate hardware failure or unauthorized reload.

5.1.10.title: VLAN Configuration Changes
5.1.10.value: VLAN changes affect segmentation. Unauthorized changes can bypass security controls.

5.1.11.title: Power Supply / Fan Failures
5.1.11.value: Hardware failures reduce redundancy. A second failure causes outage.

5.1.12.title: ARP/MAC Table Anomalies
5.1.12.value: MAC flapping indicates loops, misconfigurations, or layer-2 attacks.

5.1.13.title: ACL Deny Logging
5.1.13.value: ACL deny hits show blocked traffic. High volumes may indicate attacks or misconfigured apps.

5.1.14.title: SNMP Authentication Failures
5.1.14.value: Failed SNMP auth indicates unauthorized polling or reconnaissance.

5.1.15.title: Environmental Monitoring
5.1.15.value: Temperature alerts catch cooling failures before they cause device outages.

5.1.16.title: Route Table Flapping
5.1.16.value: Unstable routes cause packet loss and reachability failures. Detecting flapping routes prevents cascading network outages across your infrastructure.

5.1.17.title: Duplex Mismatch Detection
5.1.17.value: Duplex mismatches degrade link performance silently. They cause late collisions, CRC errors, and reduced throughput that are hard to diagnose.

5.1.18.title: CDP/LLDP Neighbor Changes
5.1.18.value: Unexpected neighbor changes indicate cabling modifications, device replacements, or unauthorized devices connecting to the network.

5.1.19.title: PoE Power Budget Monitoring
5.1.19.value: PoE budget exhaustion causes powered devices (IP phones, APs, cameras) to lose power. Proactive monitoring prevents unplanned device outages.

5.1.20.title: EIGRP Neighbor Flapping
5.1.20.value: EIGRP neighbor instability causes route recalculation, increased CPU load, and traffic blackholing during convergence.

5.1.21.title: CRC Error Trending
5.1.21.value: Increasing CRC errors indicate failing cables, SFPs, or electromagnetic interference. Early detection prevents link failures.

5.1.22.title: Syslog Source Health
5.1.22.value: Silence from a device means either it's healthy or its syslog forwarding broke. Detecting missing syslog sources ensures continuous visibility.

5.1.23.title: HSRP/VRRP State Changes
5.1.23.value: Gateway redundancy state changes impact all hosts on a subnet. Detecting unexpected failovers prevents prolonged outages.

5.2.1.title: Top Denied Traffic Sources
5.2.1.value: Identifies top blocked traffic sources — useful for rule tuning, detecting scanning, and misconfigured apps.

5.2.2.title: Policy Change Audit
5.2.2.value: Firewall rule changes can expose the network. Compliance must-have (PCI, SOX, HIPAA).

5.2.3.title: Threat Detection Events
5.2.3.value: IPS/IDS events indicate active attacks. Correlation with traffic context enables rapid response.

5.2.4.title: VPN Tunnel Status
5.2.4.value: VPN failures isolate remote sites or users. Proactive monitoring prevents "the VPN is down" calls.

5.2.5.title: High-Risk Port Exposure
5.2.5.value: Allowed traffic to RDP/SMB/Telnet from untrusted zones indicates policy gaps.

5.2.6.title: Geo-IP Anomaly Detection
5.2.6.value: Traffic to/from sanctioned or unexpected countries flags exfiltration, C2, or compromised hosts.

5.2.7.title: Connection Rate Anomalies
5.2.7.value: Sudden connection spikes indicate DDoS, scanning, or worm propagation.

5.2.8.title: Certificate Inspection Failures
5.2.8.value: SSL decryption failures mean traffic passes uninspected — could be legitimate cert pinning or SSL evasion.

5.2.9.title: URL Filtering Blocks
5.2.9.value: Shows what categories users are trying to access. Reveals policy effectiveness and shadow IT.

5.2.10.title: Admin Access Audit
5.2.10.value: Firewall admin access is highly privileged. Audit trail is a compliance must-have.

5.2.11.title: Firewall Resource Utilization
5.2.11.value: Session table exhaustion blocks new connections. CPU saturation degrades throughput.

5.2.12.title: NAT Pool Exhaustion
5.2.12.value: NAT exhaustion prevents outbound connections. Users lose internet access.

5.2.13.title: Session Table Exhaustion
5.2.13.value: When session tables fill, new connections are dropped. This causes service outages that are difficult to diagnose without firewall telemetry.

5.2.14.title: Firewall HA Failover Events
5.2.14.value: HA failovers cause brief traffic disruption and can indicate underlying hardware or link failures. Tracking failover frequency detects instability.

5.2.15.title: Botnet/C2 Traffic Detection
5.2.15.value: Detecting outbound connections to known C2 infrastructure identifies compromised internal hosts before data exfiltration occurs.

5.2.16.title: SSL/TLS Decryption Failures
5.2.16.value: Decryption failures create blind spots in security inspection. Tracking failures by destination reveals certificate pinning, protocol mismatches, or policy gaps.

5.2.17.title: Firewall Rule Hit Count Analysis
5.2.17.value: Unused firewall rules increase attack surface and complexity. Identifying zero-hit rules enables rule base cleanup and reduces risk.

5.2.18.title: Threat Prevention Signature Coverage
5.2.18.value: Outdated threat signatures leave the firewall blind to new attacks. Monitoring signature versions ensures security posture is current.

5.3.1.title: Pool Member Health Status
5.3.1.value: Offline pool members reduce capacity. All members down = complete service outage.

5.3.2.title: Virtual Server Availability
5.3.2.value: VIP down = application unreachable. Direct service impact.

5.3.3.title: Connection and Throughput Trending
5.3.3.value: Reveals application demand patterns. Useful for capacity planning and DDoS detection.

5.3.4.title: SSL Certificate Expiry
5.3.4.value: Expired certificates on load balancers cause browser warnings or connection failures. Most preventable outage.

5.3.5.title: HTTP Error Rate by VIP
5.3.5.value: Backend 5xx errors indicate application issues. Per-VIP tracking isolates degraded services.

5.3.6.title: Response Time Degradation
5.3.6.value: Increasing response times indicate backend bottlenecks before they become outages.

5.3.7.title: Session Persistence Issues
5.3.7.value: Broken persistence causes lost sessions, shopping carts, or random logouts.

5.3.8.title: WAF Policy Violations
5.3.8.value: WAF violations indicate attacks — SQL injection, XSS, command injection. Trending reveals campaigns.

5.3.9.title: Connection Queue Depth
5.3.9.value: Growing connection queues indicate backend saturation. Users experience timeouts before the server actually fails.

5.3.10.title: Backend Server Error Code Distribution
5.3.10.value: Understanding which backends return 5xx errors helps isolate faulty application instances vs. systemic issues.

5.3.11.title: Rate Limiting and DDoS Mitigation Events
5.3.11.value: Tracking rate limiting events reveals ongoing attacks and validates that DDoS protections are actively working.

5.3.12.title: iRule/Policy Errors
5.3.12.value: iRule failures cause unexpected traffic handling — potentially bypassing security or routing traffic incorrectly.

5.4.1.title: AP Offline Detection
5.4.1.value: Offline APs create coverage dead zones. Users lose connectivity in affected areas.

5.4.2.title: Client Association Failures
5.4.2.value: Failed associations frustrate users and indicate RADIUS/auth issues, RF problems, or AP overload.

5.4.3.title: Channel Utilization
5.4.3.value: High channel utilization degrades wireless performance. Identifies congested APs needing channel changes or additional coverage.

5.4.4.title: Rogue AP Detection
5.4.4.value: Rogue APs are unauthorized and can be used for man-in-the-middle attacks or network bridging.

5.4.5.title: Client Count Trending
5.4.5.value: Client count trending informs capacity planning and AP density decisions.

5.4.6.title: RF Interference Events
5.4.6.value: Radar (DFS), non-WiFi interference, and channel changes degrade wireless quality.

5.4.7.title: Wireless Authentication Trends
5.4.7.value: 802.1X success/failure rates indicate RADIUS health, certificate issues, or expired credentials.

5.4.8.title: RADIUS Authentication Failures
5.4.8.value: Mass RADIUS failures prevent wireless users from connecting. Distinguishing between user errors and server issues drives faster resolution.

5.4.9.title: Client Roaming Analysis
5.4.9.value: Poor roaming causes dropped calls, video freezes, and application timeouts. Analyzing roaming patterns identifies coverage gaps.

5.4.10.title: Wireless IDS/IPS Events
5.4.10.value: Wireless attacks (deauth floods, evil twin, KRACK) compromise network security. Early detection prevents credential theft and MitM attacks.

5.4.11.title: Band Steering Effectiveness
5.4.11.value: Band steering moves capable clients to 5 GHz, reducing congestion on 2.4 GHz. Measuring effectiveness validates RF policy.

5.5.1.title: Tunnel Health Monitoring
5.5.1.value: Tunnel loss/latency/jitter directly impacts application experience over WAN.

5.5.2.title: Site Availability
5.5.2.value: Edge device offline = remote site disconnected from the network.

5.5.3.title: Application SLA Violations
5.5.3.value: Detects when business-critical applications aren't meeting performance requirements over the WAN.

5.5.4.title: Path Failover Events
5.5.4.value: Tracks when traffic switches between WAN transports. Frequent failovers indicate unstable links.

5.5.5.title: Control Plane Health
5.5.5.value: vSmart/vManage connectivity issues affect policy distribution and overlay routing.

5.5.6.title: Certificate Expiration
5.5.6.value: SD-WAN device certificates must be valid for overlay connectivity.

5.5.7.title: Bandwidth Utilization per Site
5.5.7.value: WAN bandwidth consumption per site enables capacity planning and cost optimization.

5.5.8.title: Jitter and Latency per Tunnel
5.5.8.value: Real-time jitter and latency metrics reveal WAN quality degradation before users complain. Critical for voice/video SLAs.

5.5.9.title: Application Routing Decisions
5.5.9.value: Validates that SD-WAN policies are steering traffic correctly. Detects policy misconfigurations that route real-time traffic over suboptimal paths.

5.5.10.title: WAN Link Utilization per Transport
5.5.10.value: Unbalanced link utilization wastes expensive MPLS bandwidth while underusing broadband circuits. Enables cost-effective traffic engineering.

5.6.1.title: DNS Query Volume Trending
